docs.text: Component Minimum Recommended Python 3.10 3.11+ RAM 512 MB 1 GB+ Disk 100 MB 500 MB+ Database PostgreSQL 12+ PostgreSQL 15+

docs.title: Command Not Found
docs.text:
If dbwarden command is not found after installation:

  1. Ensure your virtual environment is activated
  2. Check if pip installed the package correctly:
pip show dbwarden
  1. Add the Python scripts directory to your PATH:
# Linux/macOS
export PATH="$HOME/.local/bin:$PATH"

# Windows
setx PATH "%PATH%;%APPDATA%\Python\PythonXX\Scripts"

docs.title: Import Errors
docs.text:
If you encounter import errors:

pip install --upgrade pip
pip install --upgrade dbwarden

docs.title: Database Driver Issues
docs.text:
Ensure you have the correct database driver installed:

# PostgreSQL
pip install psycopg2-binary

# MySQL
pip install mysql-connector-python

# SQLite (included)
# No additional driver needed

docs.title: Upgrading
docs.text:
To upgrade DBWarden to the latest version:

pip install --upgrade dbwarden

Or with poetry:

poetry update dbwarden

docs.title: Migration Files
docs.text: Migration files are executable SQL units with explicit upgrade and rollback behavior.

docs.title: Naming Convention
docs.text:
{database_name}__{version}_{description}.sql

Examples:

primary__0001_initial_schema.sql
primary__0002_add_users_table.sql
analytics__0001_create_events.sql

docs.title: File Anatomy
docs.text:
Every migration file must include two sections:

-- upgrade

-- SQL applied during migrate

-- rollback

-- SQL applied during rollback

docs.title: Migration Types
docs.text: Prefix Type Behavior NNNN_ Versioned Runs once in version order RA__ Runs always Runs each migrate execution ROC__ Runs on change Runs only when checksum changed

docs.title: Internal Execution Model
docs.text:
When migrate runs, DBWarden builds an execution plan from migration files:

def build_plan(directory, applied_versions):
    versioned = parse_versioned_files(directory)
    repeatable = parse_repeatable_files(directory)

    pending_versioned = [m for m in versioned if m.version not in applied_versions]
    pending_ra = repeatable.runs_always
    pending_roc = changed_only(repeatable.runs_on_change)

    return pending_versioned + pending_ra + pending_roc

Then each migration:

  1. Parses -- upgrade statements
  2. Executes statements in order
  3. Stores execution metadata/checksum

docs.title: Example: Versioned Migration
docs.text:
-- upgrade

CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS users (
    id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY,
    email VARCHAR(255) NOT NULL UNIQUE,
    created_at DATETIME
);

-- rollback

DROP TABLE users;

docs.title: Example: Runs-Always Migration
docs.text:
-- upgrade

CREATE OR REPLACE VIEW active_users AS
SELECT id, email FROM users WHERE is_active = TRUE;

-- rollback

DROP VIEW IF EXISTS active_users;

Filename example: primary__RA__refresh_active_users_view.sql

docs.title: Example: Runs-On-Change Migration
docs.text:
-- upgrade

CREATE OR REPLACE FUNCTION update_updated_at()
RETURNS TRIGGER AS $$
BEGIN
  NEW.updated_at = NOW();
  RETURN NEW;
END;
$$ LANGUAGE plpgsql;

-- rollback

DROP FUNCTION IF EXISTS update_updated_at();

Filename example: primary__ROC__update_timestamp_trigger.sql

docs.title: Metadata Headers
docs.text:
Dependency header:

-- depends_on: ["0004", "0005"]

Seed header:

-- seed

Headers are parsed before SQL execution and used by ordering logic.

docs.title: Best Practices
docs.text:
• Keep one logical change per migration file
  • Always write rollback SQL that actually restores previous state
  • Prefer explicit SQL over side effects in application code
  • Review generated SQL before running migrate
  • For complex data moves, use transactional blocks where backend supports it

docs.title: Validation Checklist Before Commit
docs.text:
dbwarden status -d primary
dbwarden migrate -d primary
dbwarden rollback --count 1 -d primary
dbwarden migrate -d primary

docs.title: SQLAlchemy Models
docs.text:
DBWarden can generate migration SQL from SQLAlchemy model definitions.

This page explains both usage and internal extraction behavior.

docs.title: Minimum Requirements
docs.text:
Each model should:

  1. Be a class with __tablename__
  2. Expose __table__ columns through SQLAlchemy declarative mapping
  3. Use supported SQLAlchemy column types
from sqlalchemy import Column, Integer, String
from sqlalchemy.orm import declarative_base

Base = declarative_base()


class User(Base):
    __tablename__ = "users"
    id = Column(Integer, primary_key=True)
    email = Column(String(255), unique=True, nullable=False)

docs.title: Model Discovery Internals
docs.text:
make-migrations discovers models from configured model_paths or auto-discovered models/ folders.

High-level process:

def discover_tables(paths):
    modules = load_python_modules(paths)
    tables = []
    seen = set()
    for module in modules:
        for attr in module_attributes(module):
            if is_sqlalchemy_model(attr) and attr.__tablename__ not in seen:
                tables.append(extract_table(attr))
                seen.add(attr.__tablename__)
    return tables

Duplicate table names are skipped after first discovery.

docs.title: Column Extraction Internals
docs.text:
For each SQLAlchemy Column, DBWarden extracts:

  • name
  • type string
  • nullability
  • primary key flag
  • unique flag
  • default expression
  • foreign key reference

For SQLite dev mode, DBWarden runs translation on extracted type/default fields.

docs.title: Typical App Models
docs.text:
from sqlalchemy import Boolean, Column, DateTime, ForeignKey, Integer, String, Text
from sqlalchemy.orm import declarative_base
import datetime as dt

Base = declarative_base()


class User(Base):
    __tablename__ = "users"

    id = Column(Integer, primary_key=True)
    email = Column(String(255), unique=True, nullable=False)
    is_active = Column(Boolean, default=True, nullable=False)
    created_at = Column(DateTime, default=dt.datetime.utcnow)


class Post(Base):
    __tablename__ = "posts"

    id = Column(Integer, primary_key=True)
    user_id = Column(Integer, ForeignKey("users.id"), nullable=False)
    title = Column(String(200), nullable=False)
    body = Column(Text)

docs.title: Composite Key Example
docs.text:
class OrderItem(Base):
    __tablename__ = "order_items"

    order_id = Column(Integer, ForeignKey("orders.id"), primary_key=True)
    product_id = Column(Integer, ForeignKey("products.id"), primary_key=True)
    quantity = Column(Integer, nullable=False)

docs.title: JSON + UUID Style Example
docs.text:
from sqlalchemy import JSON
from sqlalchemy.dialects.postgresql import UUID
import uuid

class Profile(Base):
    __tablename__ = "profiles"

    id = Column(UUID(as_uuid=True), primary_key=True, default=uuid.uuid4)
    settings = Column(JSON, default=dict)

In SQLite dev mode these may translate (for example UUID and JSON to TEXT).

docs.title: Type Behavior by Backend
docs.text:
DBWarden maps SQLAlchemy-like types to backend-specific SQL during generation.

  • PostgreSQL: supports SERIAL, TIMESTAMP, BYTEA
  • MySQL/MariaDB: maps booleans to compatible types
  • SQLite: simple type affinity model
  • Dev translation: can adapt non-SQLite types when running --dev with SQLite

For translation specifics, see SQL Translation.

docs.title: Practical Guidelines
docs.text:
• Keep model imports side-effect free (module import should not run app boot logic)
  • Prefer explicit nullability and defaults
  • Use manual migrations for backend-specific advanced objects (indexes, triggers, policies)
  • Review generated SQL before applying

docs.title: Troubleshooting
docs.text:
No models found:

  • Ensure model_paths points to real files/directories
  • Ensure classes define __tablename__
  • Ensure SQLAlchemy models are importable in current environment

Wrong SQL type output:

  • Check selected database config
  • If running --dev, check dev_database_url and translation behavior
  • Use --strict-translation to surface lossy conversion failures early

docs.title: Operations Runbook
docs.text: This runbook is for operating DBWarden safely in real environments.

docs.title: Scope
docs.text:
Use this guide for:

  • release migrations
  • rollback and recovery
  • lock incidents
  • checksum and drift issues

docs.title: Standard Release Procedure
docs.text:
1. Confirm target database and environment
  2. Verify pending migrations
  3. Apply migrations (with backup where needed)
  4. Verify resulting state

Commands:

dbwarden status -d primary
dbwarden migrate -d primary --with-backup
dbwarden history -d primary

For multi-db releases:

dbwarden migrate --all --with-backup

docs.title: Pre-Release Checklist
docs.text:
• Latest code pulled
  • warden.toml points to correct environment
  • Migration files reviewed (upgrade + rollback)
  • Backup strategy confirmed
  • Maintenance window/rollback owner identified

docs.title: Post-Release Verification
docs.text:
• dbwarden status -d <name> shows expected applied/pending counts
  • Application boot and health checks pass
  • Critical queries validated
  • Migration history matches deployment record

docs.title: Incident: Stuck Lock
docs.text:
Symptoms:

  • migration command reports lock already acquired
  • no active migration process is actually running

Recovery:

dbwarden lock-status -d primary
dbwarden unlock -d primary
dbwarden migrate -d primary

If lock keeps recurring:

  • check for parallel CI/CD jobs
  • enforce single migration runner per database

docs.title: Incident: Migration Failed Mid-Run
docs.text:
Symptoms:

  • command exits with SQL or transaction error
  • only part of plan applied

Recovery strategy:

  1. Inspect history and current status
  2. Identify exact failed migration file
  3. Fix SQL or environment cause
  4. Re-run migrate or rollback to safe point

Commands:

dbwarden history -d primary
dbwarden status -d primary
dbwarden migrate -d primary --verbose

docs.title: Incident: Rollback Failed
docs.text:
Symptoms:

  • rollback section invalid for current schema state

Recovery options:

  1. Create corrective manual migration using dbwarden new
  2. Apply corrective migration forward
  3. Avoid repeated failing rollback loops

Command:

dbwarden new "repair rollback state" -d primary

docs.title: Incident: SQL Translation Warning in Dev Mode
docs.text:
Symptoms:

  • warnings about unsupported type/default conversion in --dev mode

Action:

  • acceptable for local testing if fallback behavior is intentional
  • use strict mode to surface failures early

Command:

dbwarden --dev --strict-translation make-migrations "validate" -d primary

docs.title: Incident: Duplicate URL/Target Config Error
docs.text:
Symptoms:

  • startup/config errors for duplicate DB URL or target

Action:

  • ensure each configured primary/dev URL points to a unique target
  • resolve accidental overlap between sqlalchemy_url and dev_database_url

docs.title: Emergency Recovery Playbook
docs.text:
1. Stop deployment pipeline
  2. Check lock status
  3. Capture history + current status
  4. Decide: rollback vs forward-fix
  5. Execute one controlled change
  6. Re-verify system health and migration state

Suggested command sequence:

dbwarden lock-status -d primary
dbwarden history -d primary
dbwarden status -d primary

docs.title: CI/CD Guardrails
docs.text:
• run status before migrate
  • run migrations from one job only
  • fail pipeline on migration non-zero exit code
  • archive logs and migration files as artifacts

Example:

- run: dbwarden status -d primary
- run: dbwarden migrate -d primary
- run: dbwarden status -d primary

docs.title: Operational Recommendations
docs.text:
• Prefer SQLite in local --dev loops; validate release candidate against production-like DB before shipping
  • Keep migrations small and reviewable
  • Always include rollback SQL
  • Avoid editing old migration files after deployment
  • Document every manual recovery migration in release notes

docs.title: Quick Start
docs.text: This guide takes you from zero to a working migration flow in about 10 minutes.

docs.title: 1) Install
docs.text: pip install dbwarden

docs.title: 2) Initialize Project
docs.text:
From your project root:

dbwarden init

This creates warden.toml and a migrations/ directory.

docs.title: 3) Configure Databases
docs.text:
Minimal single-database config:

default = "primary"

[database.primary]
database_type = "postgresql"
sqlalchemy_url = "postgresql://user:password@localhost:5432/main"
migrations_dir = "migrations/primary"

Recommended local dev config (separate dev DB):

default = "primary"

[database.primary]
database_type = "postgresql"
sqlalchemy_url = "postgresql://user:password@localhost:5432/main"
migrations_dir = "migrations/primary"

dev_database_type = "sqlite"
dev_database_url = "sqlite:///./development.db"

docs.title: 4) Add SQLAlchemy Models
docs.text:
# models/user.py
from sqlalchemy import Column, DateTime, Integer, String
from sqlalchemy.orm import declarative_base
import datetime as dt

Base = declarative_base()


class User(Base):
    __tablename__ = "users"

    id = Column(Integer, primary_key=True)
    email = Column(String(255), unique=True, nullable=False)
    created_at = Column(DateTime, default=dt.datetime.utcnow)

docs.title: 5) Generate Migration
docs.text:
dbwarden make-migrations "create users table" -d primary

DBWarden creates a file like:

migrations/primary/primary__0001_create_users_table.sql

docs.title: 6) Review Generated SQL
docs.text:
Every migration has explicit -- upgrade and -- rollback sections.

-- upgrade

CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS users (
    id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY,
    email VARCHAR(255) NOT NULL UNIQUE,
    created_at DATETIME
);

-- rollback

DROP TABLE users;

docs.title: 7) Apply Migration
docs.text:
Production-like target:

dbwarden migrate -d primary

Local dev target:

dbwarden --dev migrate -d primary

docs.title: 8) Check State
docs.text:
dbwarden status -d primary
dbwarden history -d primary

docs.title: What Happens Internally
docs.text:
On migrate, DBWarden:

  1. Loads config and resolves target DB
  2. Creates migration metadata/lock tables if missing
  3. Acquires migration lock
  4. Parses pending files and executes statements
  5. Stores applied records and checksums
  6. Releases lock

This is why migration state stays deterministic across environments.

docs.title: Next Steps
docs.text:
• Configuration
  • Commands Overview
  • Migration Files
  • SQL Translation

docs.title: SQL Translation
docs.text:
DBWarden includes a SQL translation layer to support development workflows where your primary database differs from your development database.

The most common case is:

  • Primary database: PostgreSQL/MySQL/MariaDB/ClickHouse
  • Development database: SQLite (--dev mode)

docs.title: Why SQL Translation Exists
docs.text:
SQLite does not support all backend-specific SQL types and default expressions used by other databases.

Without translation, generated migrations can fail in local development when they contain backend-specific types like UUID, JSONB, or default expressions like now().

DBWarden translation solves this by adapting generated SQL for SQLite compatibility.

docs.title: How It Works
docs.text:
When you run commands in development mode and target a SQLite dev database:

dbwarden --dev make-migrations "sync models" -d primary

DBWarden uses this flow:

  1. Loads the selected database config and resolves dev_database_url.
  2. Detects that the active target backend is SQLite.
  3. Extracts model metadata from SQLAlchemy models.
  4. Translates backend-specific types/defaults to SQLite-compatible SQL.
  5. Generates migration SQL with translated definitions.

Translation is applied during migration generation, not as a post-processing regex pass.

docs.title: Type Translation Behavior
docs.text:
DBWarden maps many backend-specific types to SQLite-compatible types.

Examples:

  • UUID -> TEXT
  • JSON / JSONB -> TEXT
  • TIMESTAMPTZ -> DATETIME
  • SERIAL / BIGSERIAL -> INTEGER
  • Nullable(UInt64) (ClickHouse) -> INTEGER

For unsupported or unknown types that cannot be converted safely:

  • DBWarden falls back to TEXT
  • Logs a warning indicating the fallback

docs.title: Default Expression Translation
docs.text:
DBWarden also handles unsupported default expressions.

Examples of expressions that may be removed for SQLite compatibility:

  • now()
  • gen_random_uuid()
  • sequence-based defaults (like nextval(...))

In non-strict mode, unsupported defaults are removed and a warning is logged.

docs.title: Strict Translation Mode
docs.text:
If you want hard failures instead of fallback behavior:

dbwarden --dev --strict-translation make-migrations "sync models" -d primary

In strict mode:

  • Unknown/unsupported type conversions raise errors
  • Unsupported default expression conversions raise errors

Use this when you want to catch every lossy conversion early.

docs.title: Recommended Setup
docs.text:
For fast local testing and predictable developer workflows, use SQLite as your dev database:

default = "primary"

[database.primary]
database_type = "postgresql"
sqlalchemy_url = "postgresql://user:password@localhost:5432/main"
dev_database_type = "sqlite"
dev_database_url = "sqlite:///./development.db"

Then run development commands with:

dbwarden --dev migrate -d primary
dbwarden --dev make-migrations "add indexes" -d primary

docs.title: Notes and Limitations
docs.text:
• Translation focuses on compatibility for local development.
  • Some backend features cannot be represented exactly in SQLite.
  • For production accuracy, always test migrations against your production-like database too.

docs.title: check-db Command
docs.text: Inspect and display the live database schema.

docs.title: Description
docs.text: The check-db command connects to your database and displays information about its current schema, including tables, columns, indexes, and foreign keys.

docs.title: Usage
docs.text: dbwarden check-db [OPTIONS]

docs.title: Options
docs.text:
Short Long Description -o --out FORMAT Output format: txt (default), json, yaml, sql

This option is not required. Default is txt.

docs.title: Text Output (Default)
docs.text:
dbwarden check-db
# or
dbwarden check-db --out txt

docs.title: JSON Output
docs.text:
dbwarden check-db --out json
# or
dbwarden check-db -o json

docs.title: YAML Output
docs.text:
$ dbwarden check-db --out yaml
users:
  columns:
  - {default: null, name: id, nullable: false, type: INTEGER}
  - {default: null, name: username, nullable: false, type: VARCHAR(50)}
  foreign_keys: []
  indexes:
  - {columns: [email], name: idx_users_email}

docs.title: SQL Output
docs.text:
$ dbwarden check-db --out sql
-- Table: users
CREATE TABLE users (
    id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY AUTOINCREMENT,
    username VARCHAR(50) NOT NULL UNIQUE,
    email VARCHAR(255) NOT NULL UNIQUE,
    created_at DATETIME
);

-- Table: posts
CREATE TABLE posts (
    id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY AUTOINCREMENT,
    user_id INTEGER NOT NULL,
    title VARCHAR(200) NOT NULL,
    content TEXT
);

docs.title: SQL Output
docs.text:
dbwarden check-db --out sql
# or
dbwarden check-db -o sql

docs.title: Tables
docs.text:
• All tables in the database
  • Tables in specific PostgreSQL schema (if postgres_schema is set in warden.toml)

docs.title: Columns
docs.text: For each table, displays: - Column name - Data type - Nullable status - Default value

docs.title: Indexes
docs.text:
• Index name
  • Indexed columns
  • Index type (if available)

docs.title: Foreign Keys
docs.text:
• Constraint name
  • Local columns
  • Referenced table
  • Referenced columns

docs.title: Documentation
docs.text:
Generate schema documentation:

dbwarden check-db --out yaml > schema.yaml

docs.title: Debugging
docs.text:
Compare expected vs actual schema:

# Your expected schema from models
# vs actual database schema
dbwarden check-db

docs.title: Integration
docs.text:
Use in scripts with JSON output:

SCHEMA=$(dbwarden check-db --out json)
# Process with jq or other tools
echo "$SCHEMA" | jq '.users.columns | length'

docs.title: Migration Verification
docs.text:
Before and after migration checks:

# Before
dbwarden check-db > before.json

# Apply migration
dbwarden migrate

# After
dbwarden check-db > after.json

# Compare
diff before.json after.json

docs.title: Connection Used
docs.text: Uses the same database connection as other commands: - Reads from warden.toml configuration - Uses sqlalchemy_url

docs.title: Schema Inspection
docs.text:
Uses SQLAlchemy's inspect function:

from sqlalchemy import inspect
inspector = inspect(connection)
tables = inspector.get_table_names()

docs.title: Connection Failed
docs.text:
Verify database connection:

dbwarden config
# Check sqlalchemy_url is correct

# Test connection
python -c "from sqlalchemy import create_engine; engine = create_engine('$URL'); engine.connect()"

docs.title: Empty Output
docs.text: No tables found: 1. Check migrations were applied: dbwarden history 2. Verify correct database: Check connection URL 3. Check PostgreSQL schema: Set postgres_schema in warden.toml

docs.title: Best Practices
docs.text:
1. Use before migrations: Document current state
  2. Use after migrations: Verify changes
  3. Version control schema: Store check-db output in docs
  4. JSON for automation: Use JSON format in scripts

docs.title: See Also
docs.text:
• diff: Compare models vs database
  • history: View migration history
  • status: Check migration status

docs.title: database Command
docs.text: Manage multiple database configurations in warden.toml.

docs.title: Description
docs.text: The database command group provides subcommands for managing multiple database configurations from a single warden.toml file.

docs.title: Usage
docs.text: dbwarden database <subcommand> [OPTIONS]

docs.title: list
docs.text:
List all configured databases.

dbwarden database list

Output:

Databases:
  primary (default) - sqlite:///./development.db
    type: sqlite
    migrations: migrations/primary
  analytics - postgresql://user:***@localhost:5432/analytics
    type: postgresql
    migrations: migrations/analytics

docs.title: add
docs.text:
Add a new database configuration.

dbwarden database add <name> [OPTIONS]

Arguments: - name: Database name (e.g., analytics, legacy)

Options:

Short Long Description -u --url URL SQLAlchemy connection URL (required) -t --type TYPE Database type (sqlite, postgresql, mysql, mariadb, clickhouse) -m --migrations-dir DIR Migrations directory

Examples:

# Add with URL (type auto-detected from URL)
dbwarden database add analytics --url "postgresql://user:pass@localhost:5432/analytics"

# Add with explicit type
dbwarden database add legacy --url "mysql://user:pass@localhost:3306/legacy" --type mysql

# Add with custom migrations directory
dbwarden database add warehouse --url "postgresql://user:pass@localhost:5432/warehouse" --migrations-dir "migrations/warehouse"

database add validates uniqueness and rejects: - Duplicate URLs (including duplicates against any dev_database_url) - Different URLs that still resolve to the same physical database target

docs.title: remove
docs.text:
Remove a database configuration.

dbwarden database remove <name> [OPTIONS]

Arguments: - name: Database name to remove

Options:

Short Long Description -f --force Skip confirmation prompt

Examples:

# Remove with confirmation
dbwarden database remove legacy

# Remove without confirmation
dbwarden database remove legacy --force

docs.title: Adding a New Database
docs.text:
# 1. Add the database configuration
dbwarden database add analytics --url "postgresql://user:pass@localhost:5432/analytics"

# 2. Verify it was added
dbwarden database list

# 3. Generate migrations for the new database
dbwarden make-migrations "create analytics tables" -d analytics

# 4. Apply migrations
dbwarden migrate -d analytics

docs.title: Switching Between Databases
docs.text:
# Work with primary database (default)
dbwarden status

# Work with analytics database
dbwarden status -d analytics

# Generate migrations for specific database
dbwarden make-migrations "add report table" -d analytics

# Apply migrations to specific database
dbwarden migrate -d analytics

docs.title: Migrating All Databases
docs.text:
# Apply pending migrations to all configured databases
dbwarden migrate --all

# Rollback migrations on all databases
dbwarden rollback --all

docs.title: Database Context in Logging
docs.text:
When working with multiple databases, DBWarden displays color-coded database context:

  • Cyan: Database name
  • Magenta: Database type
  • Green: Success status
  • Yellow: Pending status
  • Red: Error status

Example output:

[INFO] Database: primary (postgresql)
[INFO] Applying migration: 0001_create_users.sql
[PENDING] 0001_create_users.sql
[APPLIED] 0001_create_users.sql

docs.title: Configuration
docs.text:
Databases are configured in warden.toml:

default = "primary"

[database]
[database.primary]
database_type = "sqlite"
sqlalchemy_url = "sqlite:///./development.db"
migrations_dir = "migrations/primary"

[database.analytics]
database_type = "postgresql"
sqlalchemy_url = "postgresql://user:password@localhost:5432/analytics"
migrations_dir = "migrations/analytics"

docs.title: Supported Database Types
docs.text: Type Value Notes SQLite sqlite Built-in, no drivers needed PostgreSQL postgresql Requires psycopg2-binary MySQL mysql Requires mysql-connector-python MariaDB mariadb Requires mysql-connector-python ClickHouse clickhouse Requires clickhouse-connect

docs.title: Database Not Found
docs.text:
Error: Database 'analytics' not found in warden.toml

Make sure the database is configured in warden.toml or add it first:

dbwarden database add analytics --url "postgresql://user:pass@localhost:5432/analytics"

docs.title: Missing sqlalchemy_url
docs.text:
Error: sqlalchemy_url is required for database 'analytics'

When adding a database, you must provide a connection URL:

dbwarden database add analytics --url "postgresql://user:pass@localhost:5432/analytics"

docs.title: See Also
docs.text:
• Configuration: Multi-database configuration
  • migrate: Apply migrations with -d flag
  • status: Check status for specific database
  • Databases: Supported database types

docs.title: diff Command
docs.text: Show structural differences between models and database.

docs.title: Description
docs.text: The diff command compares your SQLAlchemy model definitions against the actual database schema to identify discrepancies.

docs.title: Usage
docs.text: dbwarden diff [type]

docs.title: Arguments
docs.text: Argument Description type Type of diff: models, migrations, all (default: all)

docs.title: Options
docs.text: Option Description --verbose, -v Enable verbose logging

docs.title: Compare Models vs Database
docs.text: dbwarden diff

docs.title: Verbose Output
docs.text: dbwarden diff --verbose

docs.title: New Tables (in models, not in DB)
docs.text: Tables defined in your models but not yet created in the database.

docs.title: Missing Columns (in DB, not in models)
docs.text: Columns in the database that don't have corresponding model definitions.

docs.title: Type Mismatches
docs.text: Columns where the data type differs between model and database.

docs.title: Missing Indexes
docs.text: Indexes that exist in models (via SQLAlchemy) but not in database.

docs.title: Before Migration
docs.text:
Check what will be created:

dbwarden diff
# Shows tables/columns that need to be added

docs.title: After Migration Issues
docs.text:
Debug schema inconsistencies:

dbwarden diff
# Shows discrepancies

docs.title: Schema Drift Detection
docs.text:
Identify when database schema doesn't match code:

dbwarden diff
# Compare current state

docs.title: Requirements
docs.text:
For diff to work effectively:

  1. Models must be defined: SQLAlchemy models in models/ directory
  2. model_paths: Must be set in warden.toml or auto-discovery must find models
  3. Migrations table exists: At least one migration applied

docs.title: No Models Found
docs.text:
No SQLAlchemy models found. Please:
  1. Create models/ directory with your SQLAlchemy models
  2. Or set model_paths in warden.toml

Set model paths in warden.toml:

model_paths = ["models/", "app/models/"]

docs.title: No Migrations Table
docs.text:
No migrations table found. Run 'dbwarden migrate' first.

Apply at least one migration before using diff.

docs.title: Best Practices
docs.text:
1. Run before migrations: See what changes are pending
  2. Run after issues: Debug schema problems
  3. Use with check-db: Combine for comprehensive view

docs.title: See Also
docs.text:
• check-db: Inspect current database schema
  • make-migrations: Generate migrations from differences
  • models: SQLAlchemy model integration

docs.title: history Command
docs.text: Display the complete migration history.

docs.title: Description
docs.text: The history command shows a formatted table of all migrations that have been applied to the database, including their version, description, and when they were applied.

docs.title: Usage
docs.text: dbwarden history

docs.title: Basic Usage
docs.text:
$ dbwarden history
Migration History
================
Version | Order | Description     | Applied At           | Type
-------|-------|-----------------|----------------------|------
0001   | 1     | create users    | 2024-02-15 14:30:15 | versioned
0002   | 2     | create posts    | 2024-02-15 14:30:20 | versioned
0003   | 3     | add comments    | 2024-02-15 14:30:25 | versioned

docs.title: No Migrations Applied
docs.text: No migrations have been applied yet.

docs.title: Output Columns
docs.text: Column Description Version Migration version identifier Order Sequence number of execution Description Migration description/name Applied At Timestamp when migration was applied Type Migration type (versioned)

docs.title: What It Shows
docs.text:
• All successfully applied migrations
  • Chronological order of application
  • Execution sequence (order executed)
  • Timestamps for each migration

docs.title: Checking Applied Migrations
docs.text:
Verify which migrations are in the database:

dbwarden history

docs.title: Debugging Issues
docs.text:
When troubleshooting schema issues:

dbwarden history
# Check order and timing of migrations

docs.title: Auditing
docs.text:
For compliance and auditing:

dbwarden history > migration_audit_$(date +%Y%m%d).txt

docs.title: Compare with Local Files
docs.text:
dbwarden history     # Database state
dbwarden status     # Compare with local files

docs.title: Check Pending Migrations
docs.text:
dbwarden history     # What's applied
dbwarden status     # Plus what's pending

docs.title: Database Source
docs.text:
The history is read from the dbwarden_migrations table in your database:

SELECT * FROM dbwarden_migrations ORDER BY applied_at;

docs.title: "No migrations have been applied yet"
docs.text: This means either: 1. No migrations have been run 2. The dbwarden_migrations table doesn't exist 3. Running dbwarden migrate for the first time

docs.title: Empty History Despite Migrations
docs.text:
If migrations were applied but history is empty:

  1. Check you're connecting to the correct database
  2. Verify warden.toml configuration
  3. Check dbwarden_migrations table exists:
dbwarden check-db --out txt
# Look for dbwarden_migrations table

docs.title: Best Practices
docs.text:
1. Regular checks: Review history after deployments
  2. Documentation: Note migration purposes in descriptions
  3. Retention: Archive history for audit trails

docs.title: See Also
docs.text:
• status: Show applied and pending migrations
  • migrate: Apply migrations
  • rollback: Revert migrations

docs.title: init Command
docs.text: Initialize the DBWarden migrations directory and configuration.

docs.title: Description
docs.text: The init command creates the migrations/ directory and a warden.toml config file. This is the first command you run when setting up DBWarden in a new project.

docs.title: Usage
docs.text:
dbwarden init
dbwarden init --database primary

docs.title: Options
docs.text: Option Description --database, -d Database name to create migration directory for

docs.title: What It Does
docs.text:
1. Creates a warden.toml configuration file (if it doesn't exist)
  2. Creates a migrations/<name>/ directory for the default database
  3. Sets up the directory structure for storing migration files
  4. Does NOT create any database tables or modify the database

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
$ dbwarden init
Created configuration file: /home/user/myproject/warden.toml
DBWarden migrations directory created: /home/user/myproject/migrations/default

Next steps:
  1. Edit warden.toml with your database connection URLs
  2. Run 'dbwarden make-migrations -d <name>' to generate migrations

docs.title: Generated warden.toml
docs.text:
The command creates a starter configuration with the new multi-database format:

# DBWarden Configuration
# See documentation: https://emiliano-gandini-outeda.me/DBWarden/

# Default database
default = "default"

# Database configurations
[database]
[database.default]
# Database type: sqlite, postgresql, mysql, mariadb, clickhouse
database_type = "sqlite"
# Database connection URL (required)
sqlalchemy_url = "sqlite:///./development.db"

# PostgreSQL schema (optional, only for postgresql)
# postgres_schema = "public"

# Paths to SQLAlchemy models for auto-migration (optional)
# model_paths = ["app/models/"]

# Migration directory (optional, defaults to "migrations/<name>")
# migrations_dir = "migrations/default"

docs.title: Directory Structure
docs.text:
After running init, your project structure will look like:

myproject/
├── migrations/
│   └── default/           # Created by init command
├── models/
│   └── user.py
├── warden.toml            # Created by init command
└── app.py

docs.title: Adding Multiple Databases
docs.text:
After init, use dbwarden database add to add more databases:

dbwarden database add analytics --url "postgresql://user:pass@localhost:5432/analytics"
dbwarden database add legacy --url "mysql://user:pass@localhost:3306/legacy"

docs.title: Important Notes
docs.text:
• No database changes: This command only creates local files
  • Safe to run multiple times: Running init again is safe; it won't overwrite existing migrations or warden.toml
  • Required before other commands: Most DBWarden commands require the migrations directory to exist
  • Creates default database: The first database created is named "default" and uses SQLite

docs.title: See Also
docs.text:
• make-migrations: Generate migrations from models
  • new: Create manual migrations
  • Configuration: Full configuration guide
  • Databases: Supported databases

docs.title: lock-status and unlock Commands
docs.text: Manage migration locking to prevent concurrent execution.

docs.title: Description
docs.text: DBWarden uses a locking mechanism to ensure only one migration process can run at a time. These commands allow you to check and manage the lock state.

docs.title: lock-status
docs.text:
Check if a migration lock is currently active.

dbwarden lock-status

Output (Lock Active):

Migration lock: ACTIVE
Another migration process may be running.

Output (No Lock):

Migration lock: INACTIVE

docs.title: unlock
docs.text:
Release the migration lock (emergency use only).

dbwarden unlock

Output:

Migration lock released successfully.

Or if no lock was held:

Failed to release lock. Lock may not be held.

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: Prevent concurrent migration execution: - Avoids race conditions - Prevents schema conflicts - Ensures migration order

docs.title: How It Works
docs.text:
1. Lock is acquired before any migration operation
  2. Lock is released after completion or error
  3. Other processes wait or fail when lock is held

docs.title: Lock Table
docs.text:
DBWarden creates a dbwarden_lock table:

CREATE TABLE dbwarden_lock (
    id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY,
    locked BOOLEAN DEFAULT FALSE,
    acquired_at DATETIME,
    release_at DATETIME
);

docs.title: When Locks Are Used
docs.text: Command Acquires Lock Releases Lock migrate Yes Yes rollback Yes Yes make-migrations No No new No No history No No status No No check-db No No

docs.title: Scenario 1: Normal Operation
docs.text:
# Terminal 1
dbwarden migrate
# Lock acquired, migration runs, lock released

# Terminal 2 (while T1 running)
dbwarden migrate
# Sees lock, waits or fails

docs.title: Scenario 2: Lock Held After Error
docs.text:
# Migration fails mid-way
# Lock is NOT released
# Database may be in inconsistent state

# Check lock status
dbwarden lock-status
# Shows: ACTIVE

# Investigate issue first
# Then release if safe
dbwarden unlock

docs.title: Scenario 3: Stuck Process
docs.text:
# Process killed or crashed
# Lock remains active

# Check
dbwarden lock-status
# ACTIVE

# Force unlock (after verifying no other process)
dbwarden unlock

docs.title: Do
docs.text:
• Check lock-status before assuming deadlock
  • Investigate why lock exists
  • Ensure no other process is running
  • Document lock releases

docs.title: Don't
docs.text:
• Don't unlock without checking
  • Don't unlock if another process is running
  • Don't run concurrent migrations manually

docs.title: Lock Active During Deployment
docs.text:
# 1. Check what's running
ps aux | grep dbwarden

# 2. Check lock status
dbwarden lock-status

# 3. Wait if safe
# OR force unlock if certain
dbwarden unlock

docs.title: Lock Not Releasing
docs.text:
# Check database state
dbwarden check-db --out txt
# Look for dbwarden_lock table

# Manual lock check
psql $DATABASE_URL -c "SELECT * FROM dbwarden_lock;"

docs.title: Migration Stuck at Lock
docs.text:
# Kill stuck process
pkill -f dbwarden

# Release lock
dbwarden unlock

# Check status
dbwarden status
dbwarden history

docs.title: Force Unlock (When Safe)
docs.text:
# 1. Verify no dbwarden process running
ps aux | grep -i dbwarden

# 2. Check no connections
# PostgreSQL:
psql $DATABASE_URL -c "SELECT pid FROM pg_stat_activity WHERE state = 'active';"

# 3. Release lock
dbwarden unlock

# 4. Verify
dbwarden lock-status

docs.title: Manual Lock Release
docs.text:
If unlock fails:

-- SQL to manually release lock
UPDATE dbwarden_lock SET locked = FALSE, release_at = NOW();

docs.title: See Also
docs.text:
• migrate: Migration execution
  • rollback: Rollback execution

docs.title: make-migrations Command
docs.text: Automatically generate SQL migration files from SQLAlchemy models.

docs.title: Description
docs.text: The make-migrations command analyzes your SQLAlchemy models and generates corresponding SQL migration files. This is the primary way to create migrations when working with ORM models.

docs.title: Usage
docs.text: dbwarden make-migrations "description of changes"

docs.title: Arguments
docs.text: Argument Description description A descriptive name for the migration (optional)

docs.title: Options
docs.text:
Option Description --verbose, -v Enable verbose logging

Global options that also apply: - --dev: Use dev_database_url/dev_database_type - --strict-translation: Fail on unsupported SQL translation instead of fallback

docs.title: Basic Usage
docs.text: dbwarden make-migrations "create users table"

docs.title: With Verbose Output
docs.text: dbwarden make-migrations "add posts and comments" --verbose

docs.title: Without Description
docs.text:
dbwarden make-migrations

If no description is provided, "auto_generated" will be used.

docs.title: Development SQLite Translation
docs.text:
dbwarden --dev make-migrations "sync models" -d primary

If the dev database is SQLite, DBWarden translates unsupported backend-specific SQL types/defaults. Unsupported types that cannot be converted are emitted as TEXT with warnings.

To fail instead of fallback:

dbwarden --dev --strict-translation make-migrations "sync models" -d primary

docs.title: How It Works
docs.text:
1. Model Discovery: Automatically discovers SQLAlchemy models by:
  2. Scanning all subdirectories of the current directory
  3. Looking for models/ or model/ folders inside each subdirectory
  4. Searching parent directories (up to 5 levels)

  5. Ignoring common library folders (.venv, node_modules, etc.)

  6. Table Extraction: Reads table definitions from discovered models:

  7. Column names and types
  8. Constraints (primary key, foreign key, unique)
  9. Default values

  10. Nullable status

  11. SQL Generation: Creates two sections in the migration file:

  12. Upgrade SQL: Creates the tables/structures

  13. Rollback SQL: Drops the tables/structures

  14. File Creation: Saves the migration with naming pattern: {number}_{description}.sql

docs.title: Generated File Example
docs.text:
-- migrations/0001_create_users_table.sql

-- upgrade

CREATE TABLE users (
    id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY AUTOINCREMENT,
    username VARCHAR(50) NOT NULL UNIQUE,
    email VARCHAR(255) NOT NULL UNIQUE,
    created_at DATETIME
)

-- rollback

DROP TABLE users

docs.title: Requirements
docs.text:
Before running make-migrations:

  1. Run dbwarden init to create migrations directory
  2. Create warden.toml with sqlalchemy_url
  3. Define SQLAlchemy models with __tablename__ attribute

docs.title: Validation Checks
docs.text: The command performs several validations:

docs.title: No Models Found
docs.text:
If no models are found:

No SQLAlchemy models found. Please:
  1. Create models/ directory with your SQLAlchemy models
  2. Or set model_paths in warden.toml

docs.title: No New Migrations
docs.text:
If all models are already covered by existing migrations:

No new migrations to generate - all models already covered by existing migrations.

DBWarden automatically deduplicates SQL by checking against all existing migration files.

docs.title: Model Discovery Process
docs.text:
DBWarden searches for models in this order:

  1. Explicit paths: Paths in model_paths (comma-separated in TOML)
  2. Auto-discovery: Looks for models/ or model/ directories

The search traverses up to 5 parent directories from the current working directory.

docs.title: Supported Column Types
docs.text: SQLAlchemy Type Generated SQL Type Integer INTEGER String(n) VARCHAR(n) Text TEXT Boolean BOOLEAN DateTime DATETIME Float FLOAT Date DATE JSON JSON

docs.title: Supported Constraints
docs.text:
• Primary Key (primary_key=True)
  • Unique (unique=True)
  • Not Null (nullable=False)
  • Default values
  • Foreign Key (ForeignKey('table.column'))

docs.title: Best Practices
docs.text:
1. Descriptive names: Use clear descriptions: bash dbwarden make-migrations "add user profile fields" # NOT dbwarden make-migrations "changes"

  2. One feature per migration: Create separate migrations for different features

  3. Review generated SQL: Always review the generated SQL before applying

  4. Version control: Commit migration files to version control

docs.title: Models Not Being Discovered
docs.text:
1. Check model_paths in warden.toml
  2. Ensure models have __tablename__ attribute
  3. Verify models inherit from declarative_base()

docs.title: Incorrect SQL Generation
docs.text:
1. Check SQLAlchemy model definitions
  2. Ensure column types are compatible
  3. Review generated migration file manually

docs.title: See Also
docs.text:
• new Command: Create manual migrations
  • SQLAlchemy Models: Model requirements and best practices
  • Migration Files: Understanding migration file structure

docs.title: migrate Command
docs.text: Apply pending migrations to the database.

docs.title: Description
docs.text: The migrate command executes all pending migration files, updating the database schema to match your current migration state.

docs.title: Usage
docs.text: dbwarden migrate [OPTIONS]

docs.title: Options
docs.text:
Short Long Description -d --database NAME Target specific database --all Migrate all configured databases -c --count COUNT Number of migrations to apply -t --to-version VERSION Migrate to a specific version -v --verbose Enable verbose logging with SQL highlighting --baseline Mark migrations as applied without executing -b --with-backup Create a backup before migrating --backup-dir DIRECTORY Directory for backup files

All options are optional.

docs.title: Apply All Pending Migrations
docs.text: dbwarden migrate

docs.title: Apply with Verbose Output (Colored SQL)
docs.text:
dbwarden migrate --verbose
# or
dbwarden migrate -v

docs.title: Use Development Database Settings
docs.text:
dbwarden --dev migrate -d primary

This uses dev_database_url (and dev_database_type if configured) from warden.toml.

docs.title: Apply Specific Number of Migrations
docs.text:
dbwarden migrate --count 2
# or
dbwarden migrate -c 2

docs.title: Migrate to Specific Version
docs.text:
dbwarden migrate --to-version 0003
# or
dbwarden migrate -t 0003

docs.title: Baseline Existing Database
docs.text:
Mark existing database as migrated without executing SQL:

dbwarden migrate --baseline --to-version 0001

This is useful when: - Starting with an existing database that wasn't tracked by DBWarden - Integrating DBWarden into an existing project

docs.title: Create Backup Before Migrating
docs.text:
dbwarden migrate --with-backup
# or
dbwarden migrate -b

docs.title: Backup to Specific Directory
docs.text: dbwarden migrate --with-backup --backup-dir /path/to/backups

docs.title: Combined Options
docs.text: dbwarden migrate -c 1 -t 0002 -v -b --backup-dir ./backups

docs.title: Migrate Specific Database
docs.text:
Use -d or --database to target a specific database:

# Target database by name
dbwarden migrate -d analytics

# With verbose output
dbwarden migrate -d analytics --verbose

docs.title: Migrate All Databases
docs.text:
Use --all to apply pending migrations to all configured databases:

# Migrate all databases sequentially
dbwarden migrate --all

# With verbose output
dbwarden migrate --all --verbose

Example output with --all:

[INFO] Database: primary (sqlite)
[INFO] Migrations are up to date.

[INFO] Database: analytics (postgresql)
[PENDING] 0001_create_users.sql
Starting migration: 0001_create_users.sql (version: 0001)
[APPLIED] 0001_create_users.sql in 0.12s

[INFO] Database: legacy (mysql)
[PENDING] 0001_add_logs_table.sql
Starting migration: 0001_add_logs_table.sql (version: 0001)
[APPLIED] 0001_add_logs_table.sql in 0.08s

Migrations completed successfully across all databases.

docs.title: How It Works
docs.text:
1. Creates migrations tracking table: Creates dbwarden_migrations table if it doesn't exist
  2. Creates lock table: Creates dbwarden_lock table for concurrency control
  3. Finds pending migrations: Identifies migrations not yet applied
  4. Applies migrations: Executes each migration in order (versioned, then RA__, then ROC__)
  5. Records execution: Stores migration metadata in database

docs.title: Migrations Tracking Table
docs.text:
DBWarden creates a dbwarden_migrations table to track applied migrations:

Column Type Description id INTEGER Auto-increment ID version VARCHAR Migration version (NULL for repeatables) description VARCHAR Migration description filename VARCHAR Migration filename migration_type VARCHAR Type (versioned, runs_always, runs_on_change) checksum VARCHAR File checksum for validation applied_at DATETIME Timestamp of application

docs.title: Migration Execution Order
docs.text:
1. Versioned migrations (NNNN_*.sql): In sequential order
  2. Runs-Always migrations (RA__*.sql): Every execution
  3. Runs-On-Change migrations (ROC__*.sql): Only when checksum changes

docs.title: Locking Mechanism
docs.text:
DBWarden uses a locking mechanism to prevent concurrent migration execution:

  • Automatic lock acquisition: Lock is acquired before any migration
  • Lock release: Lock is released after completion or error
  • Lock timeout: Prevents stale locks

docs.title: Checking Lock Status
docs.text: dbwarden lock-status

docs.title: Force Unlock (Emergency)
docs.text:
dbwarden unlock

Warning: Only use unlock if you're certain no other migration process is running.

docs.title: Successful Migration
docs.text:
Pending migrations (1):
  - 0001
Starting migration: 0001_create_users.sql
Completed migration: 0001_create_users.sql in 0.05s
Migrations completed successfully: 1 migrations applied.

docs.title: Verbose Output
docs.text:
Pending migrations (1):
  - 0001
Starting migration: 0001_create_users.sql (version: 0001)
CREATE TABLE users (
    id INTEGER NOT NULL PRIMARY KEY,
    username VARCHAR(50) NOT NULL
)
Completed migration: 0001_create_users.sql (version: 0001) in 0.05s
Migrations completed successfully: 1 migrations applied.

docs.title: No Pending Migrations
docs.text: Migrations are up to date.

docs.title: Migration Execution Errors
docs.text:
If a migration fails:

  1. The migration is not recorded
  2. Database changes are rolled back (if in transaction)
  3. Error message is displayed

docs.title: Checksum Validation
docs.text:
DBWarden validates migration file checksums to ensure integrity:

  • Before execution: Verifies file hasn't been modified (for ROC__)
  • Integrity check: Compares stored checksum with current file

docs.title: Best Practices
docs.text:
1. Always use --verbose in production: Log all SQL statements
  2. Test migrations first: Run on staging before production
  3. Backup before migrating: Especially in production
  4. Don't modify applied migrations: Create new migrations instead

docs.title: Rollback Strategy
docs.text:
Before running migrations, understand your rollback options:

# Check what will be rolled back
dbwarden history

# Rollback if needed
dbwarden rollback

docs.title: GitHub Actions Example
docs.text:
name: Database Migrations

on:
  push:
    branches: [main]

jobs:
  migrate:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v3

      - name: Set up Python
        uses: actions/setup-python@v4
        with:
          python-version: '3.12'

      - name: Install dependencies
        run: |
          pip install dbwarden

      - name: Run migrations
        run: dbwarden migrate --verbose
        env:
          DBWARDEN_SQLALCHEMY_URL: ${{ secrets.DATABASE_URL }}

docs.title: Multi-Database CI/CD
docs.text:
name: Multi-Database Migrations

on:
  push:
    branches: [main]

jobs:
  migrate-all:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v3

      - name: Set up Python
        uses: actions/setup-python@v4
        with:
          python-version: '3.12'

      - name: Install dependencies
        run: |
          pip install dbwarden

      - name: Run migrations on all databases
        run: dbwarden migrate --all --verbose

docs.title: "Migrations are up to date" but you expect changes
docs.text:
1. Check migrations directory exists
  2. Verify migrations are in correct format
  3. Check dbwarden status for pending migrations

docs.title: Migration fails silently
docs.text: Run with --verbose to see detailed logs.

docs.title: Lock held by another process
docs.text:
dbwarden lock-status
# If locked, wait or use:
dbwarden unlock

docs.title: See Also
docs.text:
• database: Manage database configurations
  • rollback: Revert applied migrations
  • status: Check migration status
  • history: View migration history
  • Lock Management: Understanding migration locks
  • Configuration: Multi-database configuration

docs.title: new Command
docs.text: Create a new manual migration file.

docs.title: Description
docs.text: The new command creates a blank migration file for manual SQL authoring. Use this when you need to write custom SQL that cannot be auto-generated from models.

docs.title: Usage
docs.text: dbwarden new DESCRIPTION [OPTIONS]

docs.title: Arguments
docs.text: Argument Description description Required - A descriptive name for the migration

docs.title: Options
docs.text:
Short Long Description --version VERSION Specific version number for the migration

Note: The --version option does not have a short form. Use --version or --version VERSION.

docs.title: Basic Usage
docs.text: dbwarden new "add index to users email"

docs.title: With Custom Version
docs.text: dbwarden new "migrate data from old schema" --version 9999

docs.title: Generated File Structure
docs.text:
-- migrations/0005_add_index_to_users_email.sql

-- upgrade

-- add index to users email

-- rollback

-- add index to users email

docs.title: Dependencies
docs.text:
Specify dependencies on other migrations:

-- depends_on: ["0001", "0002"]

-- upgrade

CREATE TABLE posts (
    id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY,
    user_id INTEGER REFERENCES users(id),
    title VARCHAR(200) NOT NULL
);

-- rollback

DROP TABLE posts;

DBWarden will execute migrations in dependency order.

docs.title: Seed Migrations
docs.text:
Mark migrations as seed data (runs after all versioned migrations):

-- seed

-- upgrade

INSERT INTO service_types (name, kind) VALUES
('Web Service', 'api'),
('Database', 'db'),
('Cache', 'cache');

-- rollback

DELETE FROM service_types WHERE name IN ('Web Service', 'Database', 'Cache');

Seed migrations are useful for: - Reference data - Lookup tables - Configuration data

docs.title: When to Use Manual Migrations
docs.text:
Use new instead of make-migrations when you need to:

  • Add indexes: Performance optimizations
  • Add constraints: Advanced constraints not in models
  • Custom data migrations: Transform or clean existing data
  • Database-specific features: Use features specific to your database
  • Complex alterations: Modify multiple tables atomically

docs.title: Example: Adding an Index
docs.text:
-- migrations/0005_add_users_email_index.sql

-- upgrade

CREATE INDEX idx_users_email ON users(email);

-- rollback

DROP INDEX idx_users_email;

docs.title: Example: Data Migration
docs.text:
-- migrations/0006_normalize_usernames.sql

-- upgrade

UPDATE users
SET username = LOWER(username)
WHERE username IS NOT NULL;

-- rollback

-- No rollback needed for data normalization

docs.title: Example: Complex Schema Change
docs.text:
-- migrations/0007_add_post_status.sql

-- upgrade

ALTER TABLE posts ADD COLUMN status VARCHAR(20) DEFAULT 'draft';

CREATE TYPE post_status AS ENUM ('draft', 'published', 'archived');

ALTER TABLE posts DROP COLUMN status;
ALTER TABLE posts ADD COLUMN status post_status DEFAULT 'draft';

-- rollback

DROP TYPE post_status;
ALTER TABLE posts DROP COLUMN status;
ALTER TABLE posts ADD COLUMN status VARCHAR(20) DEFAULT 'draft';

docs.title: Version Numbering
docs.text:
If you don't specify a version, the next sequential number is auto-generated:

0001_initial_schema.sql
0002_add_users.sql
0003_add_posts.sql
0004_breaking_changes.sql

Custom versions must be 4-digit numbers:

dbwarden new "urgent fix" --version 9999

docs.title: Version Numbering
docs.text:
If you don't specify a version, the next sequential number is auto-generated:

0001_initial_schema.sql
0002_add_users.sql
0003_add_posts.sql
0004_breaking_changes.sql

Custom versions must be 4-digit numbers:

dbwarden new "urgent fix" --version 9999

docs.title: Best Practices
docs.text:
1. Descriptive names: Include what the migration does: 0005_add_users_email_index.sql # NOT 0005_index.sql

  2. Test rollback SQL: Ensure -- rollback section works correctly

  3. Idempotent migrations: Write SQL that can be run multiple times safely

  4. Document complex migrations: Add comments explaining complex operations

  5. Separate concerns: One migration per logical change

docs.title: Comparison: make-migrations vs new
docs.text: Aspect make-migrations new Source SQLAlchemy models Manual Use Case Schema from models Custom SQL Automation Full None Complexity Simple Complex Risk Lower Higher

docs.title: 1. Always Include Rollback
docs.text:
-- GOOD
CREATE INDEX idx_users_email ON users(email);
-- rollback
DROP INDEX idx_users_email;

-- BAD (no rollback)
CREATE INDEX idx_users_email ON users(email);

docs.title: 2. Use Transactions When Possible
docs.text:
-- upgrade
BEGIN;
-- your SQL here
COMMIT;

docs.title: 3. Handle Existing Data
docs.text:
-- upgrade
ALTER TABLE users ADD COLUMN new_field VARCHAR(100);

UPDATE users SET new_field = 'default' WHERE new_field IS NULL;

ALTER TABLE users ALTER COLUMN new_field SET NOT NULL;

docs.title: Version Already Exists
docs.text:
Error: Migration version already exists.

Use a different version or description.

docs.title: Empty Migration
docs.text: Ensure you write SQL in both -- upgrade and -- rollback sections.

docs.title: See Also
docs.text:
• make-migrations: Auto-generate from models
  • migrate: Apply manual migrations
  • Migration Files: Migration file format

docs.title: rollback Command
docs.text: Revert applied migrations to a previous state.

docs.title: Description
docs.text: The rollback command undoes migrations that have been applied to the database, reverting schema changes.

docs.title: Usage
docs.text: dbwarden rollback [OPTIONS]

docs.title: Options
docs.text:
Short Long Description -d --database NAME Target specific database --all Rollback all configured databases -c --count COUNT Number of migrations to rollback -t --to-version VERSION Rollback to a specific version -v --verbose Enable verbose logging

None of these options are required. All are optional.

docs.title: Rollback Last Migration
docs.text: dbwarden rollback

docs.title: Rollback Multiple Migrations
docs.text:
# Rollback last 3 migrations
dbwarden rollback --count 3
# or
dbwarden rollback -c 3

docs.title: Rollback to Specific Version
docs.text:
dbwarden rollback --to-version 0001
# or
dbwarden rollback -t 0001

docs.title: Combined Options
docs.text: dbwarden rollback -c 1 -t 0001 -v

docs.title: Rollback Specific Database
docs.text:
Use -d or --database to target a specific database:

# Rollback last migration on analytics database
dbwarden rollback -d analytics

# Rollback to specific version on analytics
dbwarden rollback -d analytics --to-version 0001 --verbose

docs.title: Rollback All Databases
docs.text:
Use --all to rollback migrations on all configured databases:

# Rollback last migration on all databases
dbwarden rollback --all

# Rollback to specific version on all databases
dbwarden rollback --all --to-version 0001 --verbose

Example output with --all:

[INFO] Database: primary (sqlite)
[INFO] Nothing to rollback on primary.

[INFO] Database: analytics (postgresql)
Rolling back migration: 0003_create_reports.sql (version: 0003)
[ROLLED_BACK] 0003_create_reports.sql in 0.05s

[INFO] Database: legacy (mysql)
[INFO] Nothing to rollback on legacy.

Rollback completed successfully across all databases.

docs.title: How It Works
docs.text:
1. Finds applied migrations: Identifies the most recent applied migrations
  2. Acquires lock: Prevents concurrent migration operations
  3. Parses rollback statements: Extracts SQL from -- rollback section
  4. Executes rollback SQL: Reverts the schema changes
  5. Records rollback: Removes migration record from tracking table

docs.title: With --count
docs.text:
dbwarden rollback --count 2

Rolls back the last 2 migrations in reverse order.

docs.title: With --to-version
docs.text:
dbwarden rollback --to-version 0001

Rolls back all migrations after and including the specified version, leaving the database at the state just before that version.

docs.title: Default Behavior
docs.text: Without options, rolls back exactly 1 migration.

docs.title: Successful Rollback
docs.text:
Rolling back migration: 0003_create_posts.sql (version: 0003)
Rollback completed: 0003_create_posts.sql in 0.03s
Rollback completed successfully: 1 migrations reverted.

docs.title: Verbose Output
docs.text:
Rolling back migration: 0003_create_posts.sql (version: 0003)
DROP TABLE posts;
Rollback completed: 0003_create_posts.sql in 0.03s
Rollback completed successfully: 1 migrations reverted.

docs.title: Nothing to Rollback
docs.text:
Nothing to rollback.

This occurs when: - No migrations have been applied - Database is at initial state

docs.title: Data Loss Warning
docs.text:
Rollback operations can result in data loss:

-- Example: Rollback drops tables
-- rollback
DROP TABLE posts;

-- Data in posts table WILL be lost!

docs.title: Irreversible Operations
docs.text:
Some operations cannot be rolled back:

  • DROP TABLE: Data is deleted
  • TRUNCATE: Data is deleted
  • ALTER TABLE (some cases): Changes cannot be undone

docs.title: Safe Rollbacks
docs.text:
Operations that can be safely rolled back:

  • CREATE INDEX: Just drop the index
  • ALTER TABLE ADD COLUMN: Drop the column
  • CREATE TABLE: Drop the table

docs.title: 1. Always Have a Backup
docs.text:
# Before rollback in production
pg_dump $DATABASE_URL > backup_$(date +%Y%m%d).sql

docs.title: 2. Test Rollbacks
docs.text:
# Test on staging first
dbwarden rollback --to-version X
# Verify application still works
# If issues, re-apply:
dbwarden migrate --to-version X

docs.title: 3. Document Rollbacks
docs.text:
Note which migrations were rolled back and why:

# Document in commit or ticket
git commit -m "Rollback posts table - bug in post creation"

docs.title: 4. Use Version Control
docs.text:
Always have migrations committed before rolling back:

git add migrations/
git commit -m "Apply migrations"
# ... issues found ...
git log --oneline -n 5

docs.title: Scenario 1: Bug in Migration
docs.text:
# Bug discovered after migration applied
dbwarden history
# 0003_create_posts.sql - has bug

# Rollback the buggy migration
dbwarden rollback --to-version 0003

# Fix the migration file
# Re-apply
dbwarden migrate

docs.title: Scenario 2: Wrong Version Applied
docs.text:
# Wrong migration applied
dbwarden status
# Shows: 0003_create_posts applied (should be 0002)

# Rollback to correct version
dbwarden rollback --to-version 0002
# Correct migrations now at 0002

docs.title: Scenario 3: Complete Reset
docs.text:
# Rollback all migrations
dbwarden rollback --count 999
# All migrations reverted to clean state

docs.title: "Nothing to rollback"
docs.text:
Check if migrations were applied:

dbwarden history
dbwarden status

docs.title: Rollback Fails
docs.text:
1. Check migration file has -- rollback section
  2. Verify rollback SQL is correct
  3. Run with --verbose for details

docs.title: Lock Held
docs.text:
dbwarden lock-status
# If locked, wait or:
dbwarden unlock

docs.title: Rollback and Migrate Together
docs.text:
You can rollback and reapply in one operation:

# Rollback and reapply
dbwarden rollback --to-version X
# Fix migration files
dbwarden migrate --to-version X

docs.title: GitHub Actions Rollback
docs.text:
name: Emergency Rollback

on:
  workflow_dispatch:
    inputs:
      version:
        description: 'Version to rollback to'
        required: true

jobs:
  rollback:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v3

      - name: Rollback
        run: dbwarden rollback --to-version ${{ github.event.inputs.version }}
        env:
          DBWARDEN_SQLALCHEMY_URL: ${{ secrets.DATABASE_URL }}

docs.title: squash Command
docs.text: Merge multiple consecutive migrations into a single migration.

docs.title: Description
docs.text: The squash command combines multiple applied migrations into one migration file, simplifying migration history.

docs.title: Usage
docs.text: dbwarden squash

docs.title: Options
docs.text: Option Description --verbose, -v Enable verbose logging

docs.title: Basic Usage
docs.text: dbwarden squash

docs.title: What It Does
docs.text:
1. Collects applied migrations: Gathers all applied migration files
  2. Combines upgrade SQL: Merges all -- upgrade sections
  3. Combines rollback SQL: Merges all -- rollback sections
  4. Creates new migration: Generates a consolidated migration file
  5. Updates tracking: Records new migration in database

docs.title: Before Squashing
docs.text:
Migrations:
├── 0001_create_users.sql
├── 0002_add_username.sql
├── 0003_add_email.sql
└── 0004_add_password.sql

docs.title: After Squashing
docs.text:
Migrations:
└── 0001_initial_schema.sql

docs.title: Cleanup Migration History
docs.text:
Reduce number of migration files:

  • Many small migrations → fewer large ones
  • Easier to review
  • Faster migration execution

docs.title: Performance Optimization
docs.text: Fewer migrations = fewer files to parse and execute.

docs.title: Requirements
docs.text:
1. All migrations applied: No pending migrations
  2. No concurrent processes: Database not in active use
  3. Backup recommended: Before squashing in production

docs.title: Downtime
docs.text: Squashing requires exclusive database access: - Lock is acquired - All migrations are reapplied - Can cause downtime in production

docs.title: Testing Required
docs.text: Always test squashing: 1. On development database 2. On staging environment 3. Before production

docs.title: Rollback Changes
docs.text: After squashing: - Old migration files are removed - New consolidated migration replaces them - Cannot easily rollback to old state

docs.title: When to Squash
docs.text:
• After many small migrations (10+)
  • Before major releases
  • During maintenance windows
  • Never in production during peak hours

docs.title: When NOT to Squash
docs.text:
• During active development
  • Before code freeze
  • If migrations are frequently changing
  • In production without testing

docs.title: Backup First
docs.text:
# Backup database
pg_dump $DATABASE_URL > backup_before_squash.sql

# Backup migrations
cp -r migrations migrations_backup

docs.title: Pending Migrations Exist
docs.text:
Cannot squash: 5 migrations are pending.
Please run 'dbwarden migrate' first.

Apply all migrations before squashing.

docs.title: Migration Table Doesn't Exist
docs.text:
No migrations found. Nothing to squash.

Apply at least one migration.

docs.title: Alternative: Manual Squashing
docs.text:
For more control, manually combine migrations:

# 1. Create new migration
dbwarden new "consolidate user migrations"

# 2. Edit the file, combining SQL from:
# - 0001_create_users.sql
# - 0002_add_username.sql
# - 0003_add_email.sql

docs.title: See Also
docs.text:
• new: Create manual migrations
  • migrate: Apply migrations
  • rollback: Revert migrations

docs.title: status Command
docs.text: Display the current migration status including applied and pending migrations.

docs.title: Description
docs.text: The status command shows a comprehensive view of your migration state, comparing migration files in the local directory with what has been applied to the database.

docs.title: Usage
docs.text: dbwarden status

docs.title: Basic Usage
docs.text:
Migration Status
================
✓ Applied   | 0001_create_users
✓ Applied   | 0002_create_posts
✓ Applied   | 0003_add_comments
Pending     | 0004_add_tags

Applied: 3
Pending: 1
Total: 4

docs.title: No Migrations Directory
docs.text: migrations directory not found. Run 'dbwarden init' first.

docs.title: No Migrations Applied Yet
docs.text:
Migration Status
================
Status    | Version | Filename
Pending   | 0001    | 0001_initial.sql

Applied: 0
Pending: 1
Total: 1

docs.title: Output Columns
docs.text: Column Description Status Migration state (Applied/Pending) Version Migration version identifier Filename Migration file name

docs.title: Status Types
docs.text: Status Icon Meaning Applied ✓ Successfully applied to database Pending (none) Not yet applied to database

docs.title: Before Applying Migrations
docs.text:
Check what's pending before deployment:

dbwarden status
# Review pending migrations
dbwarden migrate

docs.title: After Deployment
docs.text:
Verify all migrations were applied:

dbwarden status
# Confirm Applied count matches expectations

docs.title: Debugging Issues
docs.text:
When schema doesn't match expectations:

dbwarden status
# Check which migrations are pending
dbwarden migrate
# Apply any missing migrations

docs.title: Comparing with History
docs.text: Command Shows Source status Applied + Pending Local files + Database history Applied only Database only

docs.title: Summary Statistics
docs.text:
The status command provides counts:

Applied: 3     # Migrations in database
Pending: 1     # Migrations not yet applied
Total: 4       # All migration files

docs.title: Pending Migrations Exist
docs.text:
If pending migrations are shown:

dbwarden migrate --verbose

docs.title: Applied Count Doesn't Match
docs.text:
If applied count is unexpected:

  1. Check dbwarden history for applied migrations
  2. Verify database connection in warden.toml
  3. Check for migrations in wrong location

docs.title: No Migrations Directory
docs.text:
Run initialization:

dbwarden init

docs.title: Development Workflow
docs.text:
# 1. Check current state
dbwarden status

# 2. Make model changes
# ... edit models ...

# 3. Generate migration
dbwarden make-migrations "describe changes"

# 4. Check status
dbwarden status

# 5. Apply migration
dbwarden migrate

docs.title: Deployment Workflow
docs.text:
# 1. Check status before deployment
dbwarden status

# 2. Apply pending migrations
dbwarden migrate --verbose

# 3. Verify deployment
dbwarden status

docs.title: Best Practices
docs.text:
1. Always check status before applying migrations
  2. Review pending migrations in detail
  3. Compare counts before and after operations
  4. Document discrepancies if any found

docs.title: See Also
docs.text:
• history: Show applied migrations only
  • migrate: Apply pending migrations
  • make-migrations: Generate new migrations

Include version info in bug reports:

dbwarden version

docs.title: Upgrade Verification
docs.text:
After upgrading:

dbwarden version

docs.title: Versioning
docs.text:
DBWarden uses Semantic Versioning:

  • MAJOR: Breaking changes
  • MINOR: New features (backward compatible)
  • PATCH: Bug fixes (backward compatible)
